title: Inflation and Disintermediation
abstract: We test a bank credit channel through which unexpected increases in inflation lead to short-run macroeconomic fluctuations. For identification, we study an unexpected U.S. inflation increase in early 1977 and exploit differences in state-level reserve requirements for Federal Reserve nonmember banks, which create differences in banks’ inflation exposures. More exposed banks reduce lending, lowering local house prices and construction employment. We provide evidence for potential mechanisms, including a bank net wealth and a loan misallocation channel. Our results suggest that an important consequence of inflation is its impairment of the banking sector.

author.Isha Agarwal: Sauder School of Business, University of British Columbia, 2053 Main Mall, Vancouver, BC V6T 1Z2, Canada
author.Matthew Baron*: Johnson Graduate School of Management, Cornell University, 401J Sage Hall, Ithaca, NY 14853, USA

article.1. Introduction: A central question in monetary economics is whether increases in inflation lead to short-run macroeconomic fluctuations and, if so, through which channels.1 In the “neutrality of money” view, unexpected increases in inflation have little aggregate real effects even in the short run, as inflation merely changes the units of measurement. In line with this view, Shiller (1997) argues that the public's dislike of inflation mainly reflects an incorrect belief that wage growth lags price inflation. Other macroeconomic theories, however, posit reasons for why rising inflation may be “non-neutral,” causing aggregate real effects. These theories tend to focus on the role of nominal rigidities in labor markets and nonfinancial firms; however, they are ambiguous about the direction of the effect. For example, New Keynesian models generally imply that small, unexpected increases in inflation can increase output and employment by relaxing nominal wage rigidity constraints (e.g., Ball et al., 1988). Macroeconomic models with financial frictions also predict that small, unexpected increases in inflation can increase output by reducing the real debt burden of financially constrained agents (e.g., Bernanke et al., 1999). These classes of theories thus have a difficult time explaining “stagflation,” simultaneous high inflation and slow economic growth. On the other hand, in models stressing investment uncertainty, tax distortions, or non-indexation of contracts, increases in inflation can have a negative impact on economic performance (e.g., Auerbach, 1979; Ball and Cecchetti, 1990; Feldstein, 1997).2This paper argues that much of the prior academic research and policy work on inflation, both regarding the U.S. and international settings, has overlooked a key class of channels—banking channels—through which rising inflation can lead to short-run macroeconomic fluctuations. Banking channels have been widely explored in empirical financial research as key propagation mechanisms through which monetary policy or shocks to bank net wealth can lead to changes in output and employment (e.g., Kashyap and Stein, 2000; Jiménez et al., 2012, 2014; Chodorow-Reich, 2014). Higher nominal interest rates caused by rising inflation might therefore be a major source of impairment to the banking sector, either due to banks’ interest rate mismatch or through other mechanisms. This paper's main contribution is to link these widely understood banking channels from the finance literature to the macroeconomic discussion of inflation, which has, for the most part, abstracted away from the role of the financial sector. To do so, our paper exploits a quasi-experimental setting from the U.S. in the 1970s to demonstrate how rising inflation can be contractionary for both bank lending and the macroeconomy. As a result, our paper—along with a contemporaneous paper by Dreschler et al., (2020)—provides a compelling mechanism to help explain stagflation in both U.S. and international settings.Our main hypothesis, that rising inflation tends to be contractionary for bank lending, is motivated by two initial descriptive findings that we uncover. First, in an unbalanced country-level panel from 1870 to 2016, we find that large increases in inflation are correlated with future short-run decreases in bank credit-to-GDP. Second, within historical and international inflation episodes for which we obtain bank-level data—ranging from France and Germany in the 1920s to emerging economies in recent decades—we find that the lending contraction is driven primarily by the banks that are most exposed to inflation increases, as measured by a balance-sheet–based measure of a bank's inflation exposure described below.However, we view these results as descriptive findings to motivate our hypothesis, given several identification challenges in interpreting such findings. One issue is that rising inflation may in itself cause little macroeconomic harm but may simply be a symptom of underlying problems, such as fiscal imbalances, supply-side disruptions, or a currency depreciation. Although the second descriptive finding helps isolate away aggregate factors by relying only on cross-sectional comparisons of banks that have different exposure to rising inflation, it highlights a second identification challenge that we face, which is that highly inflation-exposed banks might be systematically different from other banks in a way that is correlated with their subsequent lending.To overcome these challenges, we develop a novel identification strategy based on state-level reserve requirements to analyze banks in the U.S. in early 1977, when the inflation rate saw a one-time increase from 5% to 7%, after which it plateaued for the subsequent year. Although this increase in inflation in early 1977 was not as large as separate and more prominent inflation increases in 1973–1974 and 1978–1981, this setting has a variety of unique identification advantages, discussed below, helping to isolate rising inflation from other factors that can affect bank lending.Using balance-sheet data from individual banks, we construct an inflation exposure measure of each bank's asset-liability inflation mismatch, which captures the sensitivity of the real value of bank equity to unexpected shocks to future inflation expectations. To generate plausibly exogenous variation in inflation exposure in the cross-section of banks, we exploit across-state differences in reserve requirements for Federal Reserve (Fed) nonmember banks, along with within-state differences between Fed nonmember versus member banks. As reserve requirements primarily mandate a minimum cash-to-demand-deposit ratio, we show that higher reserve requirements substantially increase banks’ inflation exposures, since the real value of non-interest-bearing cash is eroded when inflation unexpectedly rises.Specifically, our empirical approach adopts Duflo's (2001) instrumental variables (IV) framework with a differential treatment effect to analyze the effect of the early-1977 inflation increase. In this IV framework, as Fed nonmember banks are all state-chartered and have reserve requirements that differ by states, nonmember banks receive a “treatment” (according to state-level reserve requirements) that differs in intensity across states, which in turn affects their inflation exposures. In contrast, member banks, whether nationally or state-chartered, have uniform reserve requirements across all states (i.e., no variation across states) and thus serve as a “nontreated” control group. Intuitively, we think of the variation in inflation exposure as mainly driven by the across-state differences for nonmember banks due to differences in state-level reserve requirements; however, by also effectively differencing out nonmember versus member banks within the same state, our identification helps ensure that our results are not driven by different macroeconomic conditions or other differences between states.A formal first-stage analysis shows that nonmember banks with higher state-level reserve requirements have higher inflation exposures in December 1976 (i.e., prior to the 1977 inflation increase), as measured by the bank-level balance-sheet measure of inflation asset-liability mismatch. In contrast, for member banks (the nontreated control group), there is no effect on inflation exposure. In the second stage, we find that banks with the most positive inflation exposures (as instrumented by higher state-level reserve requirements) reduce lending the most after the inflation increase. The estimated aggregate effect is that U.S. loan growth is reduced by 3.2 percentage points, relative to average loan growth of 19% in 1977.We show that our results also hold more generally in an ordinary least squares (OLS) setting of all U.S. commercial banks from 1976 to 2019. However, our study mainly focuses on the early-1977 episode, which has several identification advantages. First, unlike other episodes of accelerating inflation, early 1977 experienced a one-time jump in the inflation rate—from 5% to 7%—where inflation subsequently remained for a year, and, importantly for us, the jump was immediately recognized by markets and priced one-for-one into higher nominal short-term rates (but with minimal change in ex post real short-term rates). Second, this burst of inflation is generally attributed to a single primary cause: an increase in energy prices early in the year due to an abnormally cold winter, which quickly filtered into broadly higher prices. Third, there were no large currency changes, long-term interest rate movements, or expectations of changes in monetary policy.3 In contrast, the more prominent 1973–1974 and 1978–1981 inflation episodes coincided with several confounding factors, including substantial changes in monetary policy and large changes in the dollar exchange rate. Fourth, there are data advantages: a) The Call Reports data is only available electronically starting in 1976, precluding any analysis of previous inflation episodes; b) The Monetary Control Act abolished state-level reserve requirements in 1980, precluding our identification strategy after 1980; and c) State-level reserve requirements are comprehensively reported only for 1976 (by Gilbert and Lovati, 1978).We then show that highly inflation-exposed banks primarily reduce lending to households (which is primarily mortgage lending), and we use state-level aggregate data to show that states with high-inflation-exposed banks see reductions in house price growth and construction employment. We do not see any state-level effects on other types of employment. These results suggest that the credit supply decrease from inflation is transmitted to the macroeconomy in large part through a housing effect. Our results are consistent with a large body of literature demonstrating that banking channels tend to strongly affect construction employment and housing (e.g., Bernanke and Gertler, 1995).We explore three specific banking channels through which higher inflation might affect bank lending: a bank “net wealth” channel, a “loan misallocation” channel, and a “deposit outflows” channel. Consistent with a bank net wealth channel, in which impaired equity or earnings lead banks to reduce loan growth, we show that more inflation-exposed banks earn lower net interest margins, as higher inflation forces these banks to pay higher interest expenses on interest-bearing liabilities without receiving similarly higher interest income. The estimated decrease in return-on-equity for the average bank is 2.5 percentage points, compared to an average return-on-equity of 10.5%.We also find evidence consistent with a loan misallocation channel, in which higher expected inflation volatility prompts the most inflation-exposed banks to shorten the maturity of their loan portfolio and reallocate their assets away from long-term nominal loans and toward inflation-protected assets. Furthermore, we see that inflation-exposed banks primarily reduce loans to households (which are primarily long-term nominal mortgage loans), rather than commercial and industrial (C&I) loans (which are primarily short-term loans)—consistent with this channel. This finding helps explain how the real effects we find are concentrated in construction employment and house prices, rather than in other sectors.We next investigate the deposit outflows channel, in which rising inflation can lead to an aggregate outflow of deposits due to regulatory ceilings on time- and savings-deposit rates, as savers take their funds outside the banking system to market-rate investments that earn higher nominal rates. Drechsler (2020) argue the importance of this channel in explaining accelerating inflation in 1978–1981. However, we find little evidence for this channel in 1977—in particular, we see little change in net inflows of either demand deposits or time and savings deposits, be it in the time series or cross-section—and we show that deposit rate ceilings did not begin to substantially bind until early 1978.Our paper complements a growing body of literature exploring the linkages among inflation dynamics, the pricing of financial instruments, and corporate financing (e.g., Kang and Pflueger, 2015; Rey, 2016; Du, 2020). Section 2 presents our theoretical framework, Section 3 our data, Section 4 our international findings, and Section 5 our main results. Section 6 concludes.
article.2. Theoretical framework: 2.1. Model overviewIn this section, we provide an overview of a theoretical model, which we present in full in Appendix Section A and which illustrates how a higher expected level of inflation or higher expected inflation volatility can affect bank lending. The model is based on the canonical work of Gray (1978), who studies optimal labor contracts in the presence of volatile inflation, which we adapt to the case of the banking market. Although our work builds on three well-established banking mechanisms—“net wealth,” “loan misallocation,” and “deposit outflows” channels—our contribution is not to present these as new channels, as all these mechanisms work through inflation's impact on nominal interest rates and banks’ nominal interest rate exposures. Rather, the goals of the model are threefold. First, the model theoretically delineates these three channels and makes testable predictions for each in the case of the 1977 inflation increase. Second, it gives theoretical justification for our empirical measure of inflation exposure presented in Section 3. Third, the model implicates the role of various institutional frictions and predicts why certain channels tend to dominate in some, but not other, institutional settings; see Section 2.2.Before presenting the model, it is important to understand repricing of bank asset and liability items with rising inflation. In the U.S. today, where the Fed has tight control over short-term interest rates, a rise in interest rates is often due to an increase in the Federal Funds target rate as a deliberate response to inflation. However, even in the absence of monetary policy actions, nominal yields on long-term securities and long-term lending rates can rise endogenously with higher expected inflation, affecting bank balance sheets and future lending decisions. Thus, we are not simply arguing that the lending contraction occurs through standard monetary policy channels due to a deliberate tightening of monetary policy. In many settings, even short-term nominal rates can rise without monetary policy action for two reasons. First, many central banks have, or historical had, primary operational targets other than interest rates, targeting mainly monetary aggregates (e.g., bank reserves or M1) or exchange rates (e.g., pegs), leaving interest rates to float freely (Bindseil, 2014). In the U.S. 1977 setting, the Fed did not publicly target a constant Federal Funds rate, as they do today but instead had a variety of targets for monetary aggregates like M1.4 As a result, the rise in short-term interest rates in early 1977 was endogenous, one-for-one with inflation, without any change in the Federal Open Market Committee's (FOMC) monetary policy stance (Romer and Romer 1989). Second, in many emerging markets, central banks only have weak control over broader short-term interest rates, due to underdevelopment of the financial system (e.g., lack of liquidity in interbank markets, lack of high-quality securities to purchase). As a result, central banks often struggle with interest rate control in inflationary settings, resulting in volatile short-term rates (Van ’t dack, 1999).Our model features an optimizing, risk-averse bank that operates in competitive markets for long-term lending and market-rate short-term borrowing. The bank's objective is to maximize the expected net present value of future earnings, while avoiding violating a reserve requirement that mandates that the bank must keep at minimum fraction ϕ of its assets in non-interest-bearing cash; otherwise, the bank incurs a regulatory penalty ψ proportional to assets. The bank makes long-term fixed-rate loans, which are funded by demand deposits and by market-rate short-term funding, and makes optimal choices for the following variables: the quantity of loans L, loan maturity M, real lending rate i, fraction of inflation-indexed loans γ, and non-interest-bearing cash holdings c.5 The bank faces both a demand curve for new loans (i.e., borrowers will demand more loans at lower real lending rates i) and a supply curve for market-rate short-term funding (i.e., money markets will supply more funds at higher real funding rates r).The bank exists within a continuous-time, infinite-horizon world. The inflation rate is initially zero but jumps permanently just after t = 0 to rate V˜, where V˜ is a normally distributed random variable with mean 0 and volatility σ; thus, σ captures expected inflation rate volatility. New lending or rolling over an existing loan entails a per-period transaction cost k, and inflation indexation entails a fixed per-period transaction cost π per loan (both exogenous parameters). The bank also has a leverage constraint, in which bank equity losses can force the bank to reduce assets and loans in proportion to the loss in book equity.We can then characterize the optimal lending, indexing, and maturity decisions of the bank in the presence of expected inflation volatility. We start with the optimal fraction of indexed loans, γ. The optimal γ reflects a trade-off between the transaction costs of indexation and the expected risk of bank losses generated by inflation, which could lead the bank to violate its reserve requirement; it is increasing in inflation volatility σ and decreasing in the indexation cost π and reserve requirement ϕ. The first key result from our model, consistent with Gray's (1978) model, is that γ is either zero or one: Banks only index their loans when the expected volatility of inflation exceeds a given cutoff. This first result helps explain why inflation-indexed loans are only offered in countries with very high and volatile inflation rates—such as Brazil, Chile, Israel, and Iceland in the 1970s—but not generally elsewhere.6We next discuss the optimal choice of loan maturity M, given γ = 0.7 Our model predicts that, despite the high exposure of long-term fixed rate loans to potential losses from future inflation increases, banks will make long-term fixed-rate loans with staggered maturities in equilibrium, even when they expect volatile inflation. The reason is that banks, in choosing the optimal loan maturity, must balance the trade-off between minimizing their exposure to inflation (which favors short contract duration) with the transaction cost C of rolling over loans more frequently (which favors long contract duration).8Given the presence of long-term fixed rate loans, banks lose value when inflation increases. This is because market rate short-term funding instantly becomes nominally more expensive with higher inflation, but long-term fixed rate loans can be refinanced only after they are due with maturity M. Given the leverage constraint, the reduction in bank value forces the bank to cut lending. (See Equation (A.16).) This is the net wealth channel. The empirical prediction from this channel is that the most inflation-exposed banks will see a fall in net interest margins and a reduction in loan quantity. The model helps us understand why bank losses are unavoidable in equilibrium even when banks expect high inflation volatility: The transaction costs of rolling over loans or institutional factors favoring long-term loans make it suboptimal for banks to completely insulate their balance sheets from future inflation increases.A comparative statics exercise, in which we vary the expected volatility of inflation, highlights our second channel, the loan misallocation channel. Comparing the previous equilibrium to a new equilibrium with higher expected inflation volatility σ, we see that banks optimally shorten the maturity of their portfolios and reduce their long-term nominal lending (see Equations (A.11) and (A.14)) as they rebalance toward a new, shorter-maturity optimal asset portfolio. For the U.S. in 1977, due to institutional arrangements in the mortgage market, this channel entails a shift away from residential mortgages (which tend to be long-term fixed-rate loans) and toward short-term securities and C&I loans (which have on average short maturities in the U.S.). The empirical prediction of this second channel that we test is that an increase in expected inflation volatility will lead the most inflation-exposed banks to shorten their average loan maturity, shift away from mortgage lending, and reduce their overall inflation exposures.Note that the relative strength of the net wealth versus misallocation channels depends on whether the increase in inflation expectations is a shift in level or in expected inflation volatility. Often, the two can coincide, as inflation increases often cause banks to update their expectations about future inflation volatility, thus, also activating the loan misallocation channel, as we discuss further in Section 5.6.The third channel, the deposit outflows channel, can be important in the presence of another common and important institutional friction, ceilings on deposit rates, or financial repression, more generally. This channel arises in a special case in our model in which the supply curve for market-rate short-term funding is constrained by regulatory rate ceilings, which gives rise to outflows in bank funding. Empirically, rising inflation can lead to an aggregate outflow of deposits due to regulatory ceilings on time- and savings-deposit rates, as savers take their funds outside the banking system to market-rate investments that earn higher nominal rates.Finally, the model gives theoretical justification for our empirical measure of inflation sensitivity in Section 3, which, as the model shows, is a key parameter in both the net wealth and loan misallocation channels. It says that the sensitivity of bank equity to inflation can be measured as −1δ[δ2M(1−γ)LoansAssets+CashAssets−DemandDep.Assets−EquityAssets], where δ is the bank's discount rate. We define the term in the square brackets as the theoretical “inflation exposure,” which corresponds to our empirical measure in Section 3 for a bank with the stylized balance sheet in the model under the assumption that δ2M≈1. In particular, the model shows that a bank with higher reserve requirements has higher inflation sensitivity and, thus, is more exposed to both the net wealth and loan misallocation channels.2.2. Why do different channels tend to dominate in different institutional settings?The model makes predictions about which channels dominate, primarily based on three characteristics: expected inflation volatility, changes in the level of expected inflation, and regulation (especially reserve requirements and ceilings on deposit rates). If expected inflation volatility is low or moderate, then banks will be relatively unhedged to future inflation, as the cost of indexation outweighs the benefits. Thus, in this scenario, the strength of the net wealth channel will vary based on the shock to the level of expected inflation, while the loan misallocation channel will vary based on the shock to expectations of inflation volatility. If, additionally, there are ceilings on deposit rates, then the deposit outflows channel can prevail, but only if market interest rates move substantially above the rate ceilings.We conjecture that the scenario above generally describes the setting in the U.S. in 1976, when expected inflation volatility was moderate. We defer a more detailed discussion to Section 5.6, after we present the institutional context in greater detail. As a preview, the predicted importance of the net wealth channel and loan misallocation channel crucially depends on whether inflation expectations were increasing or decreasing prior to 1977—and the historical record, we argue, presents conflicting evidence. We thus leave it as an empirical question to what extent these two channels are present in the data, noting that these channels make specific empirical predictions—notably, the loan misallocation channel predicting the shortening of average loan maturity and a reduction in a bank's inflation exposure—which we investigate in Section 5.6.What does the model predict in the case of international inflation episodes when expected inflation volatility is unusually high? In the absence of any regulatory frictions, we would expect little effect from either the net wealth or the misallocation channel, as the framework predicts that banks are already hedged either through inflation indexation of loans or by having a short-net-duration balance sheet. However, the absence of regulatory frictions in high-inflation countries is rarely the case: High-inflation countries often institute extensive financial repression in the form of regulated lending rates, deposit rate ceilings, and especially reserve requirements that force banks to hold sovereign debt and effectively finance government spending. In this case, high financial repression will likely make both the net wealth channel and the deposit outflows channel important: Bank equity will be highly exposed to inflation due to rigid bank portfolio constraints (the net wealth channel), and depositors will divert savings to market-rate or foreign-currency vehicles (the deposit outflows channel) when inflation rises. In contrast, the loan misallocation channel is likely less relevant with high financial repression, as banks have little remaining balance-sheet discretion, having already hedged their inflation risk to the maximum extent possible under such constraints.
